items.subtopic: Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Blood Pressure Regulation
items.content: Blood pressure is controlled by cardiac output and peripheral resistance, with the renal-volume-endocrine system playing a crucial role in long-term regulation. This involves manipulation of blood volume and total peripheral resistance.
keyPoints: Arterial BP is controlled by cardiac output and peripheral resistance.
Renal-volume-endocrine pressure control system adjusts arterial BP.
Cardiac output influenced by stroke volume and heart rate.
Peripheral vascular resistance determined by small arteries and arterioles.
items.reference: Hypertension · Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Role of Endothelial Function in Hypertension
items.content: Endothelial cells regulate vascular tone and blood flow by releasing various factors. Dysfunction in these cells can contribute to hypertension and atherosclerosis.
keyPoints: Endothelial cells synthesize relaxing and contracting factors like nitric oxide.
Endothelial dysfunction may initiate or progress hypertension and atherosclerosis.
Vascular tone is regulated by endothelial-derived vasoactive hormones.
items.reference: Hypertension · Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Pressure-Natriuresis Mechanism
items.content: The pressure-natriuresis mechanism describes how increased arterial pressure from high sodium intake leads to increased urinary sodium excretion, maintaining sodium balance at the cost of elevated blood pressure.
keyPoints: High sodium intake increases arterial pressure and urinary sodium excretion.
Pressure-natriuresis involves glomerular filtration rate changes and hormonal factors.
Impaired sodium excretion requires higher arterial pressure for natriuresis.
items.reference: Hypertension · Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.title: Mechanisms of Antihypertensive Drugs
items.content: Antihypertensive drugs manage high blood pressure by targeting various physiological mechanisms. Direct vasodilators and calcium channel blockers induce arteriolar vasodilation, while RAAS-blocking agents like ACEIs, ARBs, and renin inhibitors counteract angiotensin II's vasoconstrictive effects and aldosterone's sodium-retaining properties. Beta blockers reduce blood pressure by impairing sympathetic neurotransmitter actions on vasoconstriction and heart rate.
keyPoints: Direct vasodilators and calcium channel blockers cause arteriolar vasodilation.
RAAS-blockers (ACEIs, ARBs, renin inhibitors) inhibit angiotensin II's vasoconstrictive effects and aldosterone's sodium retention.
Beta blockers impair sympathetic neurotransmitter actions on vasoconstriction and heart rate.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.title: Diuretics in Blood Pressure Management
items.content: Diuretics are crucial in blood pressure management by affecting electrolyte and fluid exchange in the kidneys. Thiazide diuretics, which enhance sodium excretion in the distal convoluted tubule, reduce intravascular volume and peripheral arteriolar resistance over time. Loop diuretics act on the loop of Henle for potent sodium excretion but are less effective for long-term BP control due to their short-acting nature.
keyPoints: Thiazide diuretics enhance sodium excretion in the distal convoluted tubule, reducing intravascular volume and peripheral resistance over time.
Loop diuretics produce potent sodium excretion in the loop of Henle but are less effective for long-term BP control due to short-acting nature.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.title: Why Standardized BP Measurement is Crucial for Accurate Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Standardizing blood pressure (BP) measurement procedures ensures accurate assessment, which is critical for diagnosing hypertension and estimating cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk. This standardization helps avoid systematic errors that can lead to falsely high or low BP estimates.
keyPoints: Office and clinic BP measurements are essential for assessing BP and CVD risk.
Standardized procedures were developed based on quantifying factors leading to measurement errors.
BP varies randomly, necessitating multiple readings over several visits for accurate classification.
Use a clinically validated oscillometric BP device to minimize error.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.title: How to Accurately Measure Office Blood Pressure
items.content: Accurate office BP measurement involves several key steps, including patient preparation and the use of appropriate equipment. These steps help ensure that readings reflect true BP levels.
keyPoints: Instruct patients to avoid caffeine, smoking, alcohol, or exercise 30 minutes before measurements.
Use a quiet room with comfortable temperature for the procedure.
Rest the patient for 3–5 minutes before taking the first measurement.
Seat the patient properly and use an appropriately sized cuff at heart level.
Measure BP using a validated device by a trained healthcare professional.
Average readings from multiple visits to estimate usual BP levels.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Classification of Hypertension in Adults
items.content: Hypertension classification for adults is based on systolic and diastolic blood pressure (BP) readings. The American College of Cardiology (ACC) and American Heart Association (AHA) guidelines recommend using the higher category when SBP and DBP fall into different categories.
keyPoints: Hypertension in adults ≥18 years is defined as an average of three SBP or DBP readings at or above 130/80 mmHg, respectively.
When SBP and DBP are in different BP categories, the higher classification should be chosen.
items.reference: Hypertension · DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Importance of Accurate Blood Pressure Measurement
items.content: Accurate blood pressure measurement is crucial for correct hypertension diagnosis. Common errors include overestimation, leading to an inflated prevalence rate, and underestimation, resulting in missed diagnoses.
keyPoints: SBP is often overestimated by ~7 mmHg, causing a 15–20% overestimation of hypertension prevalence.
Accurate BP measurement requires averaging two or more readings from at least two occasions.
Trained nonphysician staff can efficiently obtain high-quality BP measurements.
items.reference: Hypertension · DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.title: Importance of Out-of-Office BP Measurements in Hypertension Management
items.content: Out-of-office blood pressure (BP) measurements, such as home or ambulatory monitoring, are crucial for accurately diagnosing and managing hypertension. They help identify white coat hypertension and masked hypertension, which can significantly impact cardiovascular risk assessment and treatment strategies.
keyPoints: White coat hypertension prevalence: 15–25%
Masked hypertension prevalence: 15–25%
Home BP measurement: average of two to three readings in the morning and evening over about 3 days
BP for hypertension recognition: SBP ≥130 mmHg, DBP ≥80 mmHg
BP control target: SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg
Ambulatory BP equivalent readings for office average of 130/80 mmHg: 130/80 (daytime), 110/65 (nighttime), 125/75 (24-h)
Non-dippers have higher CVD risk, more common in non-Hispanic blacks
items.reference: Hypertension · OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.title: Role of Home BP Monitoring in Hypertension Management
items.content: Home blood pressure monitoring is a practical approach for obtaining out-of-office BP readings. It engages patients in their care and provides accurate data to guide treatment decisions, especially useful for confirming office hypertension or detecting masked hypertension.
keyPoints: Use clinically validated devices
Average two to three morning/evening measurements over 3 days
BP control target: SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg
Home BP readings can be transferred electronically to reduce bias
items.reference: Hypertension · OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.title: Ambulatory BP Monitoring for Hypertension Diagnosis and Risk Assessment
items.content: Ambulatory BP monitoring offers detailed insights into BP patterns, including nighttime readings, which are predictive of cardiovascular disease risk. It is particularly useful in confirming hypertension diagnoses where uncertainty exists.
keyPoints: Equivalent ambulatory BP readings: 130/80 (daytime), 110/65 (nighttime), 125/75 (24-h)
Non-dippers have higher CVD risk
More common in non-Hispanic blacks
Useful for confirming diagnosis, especially with uncertainty
items.reference: Hypertension · OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Understanding the Age-Related Increase in Hypertension Prevalence
items.content: Hypertension prevalence increases with age, largely due to physiological changes such as a progressive increase in systolic blood pressure (SBP) until about the eighth decade of life and isolated systolic hypertension late in life.
keyPoints: Age-related increases in BP are noted globally.
SBP increases progressively until about the eighth decade.
DBP increases with age but remains stable or declines after the fifth decade.
Isolated systolic hypertension is common due to widening pulse pressure.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Hypertension Prevalence and Racial/Ethnic Disparities in the U.S.
items.content: The prevalence of hypertension varies significantly by age, race/ethnicity, and region within the United States, with non-Hispanic black adults experiencing higher rates and earlier onset compared to other groups.
keyPoints: >103 million U.S. adults have hypertension according to ACC/AHA criteria.
Prevalence is ~20–30% in adults aged 20–44 years and increases to 80–85% in those aged 75+.
Non-Hispanic black adults have a prevalence of 59%, higher than whites (45%), Hispanics (47%), or Asians (46%).
Hypertension begins at a younger age in non-Hispanic blacks, leading to more cardiovascular and kidney complications.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Global Trends in Hypertension Prevalence
items.content: Hypertension is a global health issue with varying prevalence across different income countries, showing an increasing trend in low- and middle-income countries.
keyPoints: Prevalence of hypertension is very common worldwide.
In 2010, the prevalence was estimated at 31.5% (1.04 billion) in low- and middle-income countries.
High-income countries had a prevalence of 28.5% (349 million).
Trends show an increase in low- and middle-income countries and a slight decline in high-income countries.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK
items.title: Importance of Basic Laboratory Testing in Newly Diagnosed Hypertension
items.content: Basic laboratory tests are crucial for assessing target organ damage, identifying secondary causes of hypertension, recognizing comorbid conditions, estimating atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk, and guiding antihypertensive therapy.
keyPoints: Complete blood count, serum electrolytes (sodium, potassium, calcium), serum creatinine and eGFR, lipid profile, glycemic status (hemoglobin A1c or fasting blood glucose), thyroid-stimulating hormone level, urinalysis, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, 12-lead ECG are recommended.
These tests help in identifying kidney disease, primary aldosteronism, diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia.
items.reference: Hypertension · LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK

items.subtopic: LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK
items.title: Continuous Risk Relationship Between Blood Pressure and Cardiovascular Disease
items.content: Blood pressure (BP) is associated with cardiovascular disease risk in a continuous, log-linear fashion. Lower BP levels are generally better for preventing atherosclerotic complications.
keyPoints: Risk relationship documented from SBP as low as 90 mmHg to over 180 mmHg.
Lower BP may be optimal to prevent BP-related atherosclerotic complications.
items.reference: Hypertension · LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK

items.subtopic: ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK
items.title: Guidelines for ASCVD Risk Estimation in Hypertensive Patients Without Prior CVD
items.content: For U.S. adults aged 40–75 years, use the ACC/AHA pooled cohort equations to estimate ASCVD risk. Consider patients with a 10-year risk ≥10% as high risk.
keyPoints: Use ACC/AHA pooled cohort equations for U.S. adults 40–75 years old.
Patients with a 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10% are considered at high risk.
items.reference: Hypertension · LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.title: Environmental Factors in Primary Hypertension
items.content: Primary hypertension is influenced by several environmental factors, including air pollution and dietary habits. Air pollutants such as particulate matter (PM2.5) are associated with an increase in systolic blood pressure (SBP) of approximately 3–5 mmHg. Additionally, unhealthy diets high in sodium and low in potassium, physical inactivity, excess body weight, and alcohol consumption contribute significantly to elevated BP levels.
keyPoints: Air pollution exposure can raise SBP by ~3–5 mmHg.
Dietary factors: High sodium intake and low potassium intake are linked with higher BP.
Physical inactivity and obesity (BMI ≥25 kg/m²) are prevalent risk factors for increased BP.
Alcohol consumption is common, with >60% of U.S. adults consuming alcohol annually.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.title: Dietary and Lifestyle Contributions to Hypertension
items.content: Unhealthy dietary patterns and lifestyle choices are major contributors to primary hypertension. High sodium intake, insufficient potassium consumption, lack of physical activity, and excessive alcohol use are strongly associated with higher blood pressure levels. These factors also correlate with cardiovascular disease (CVD) complications.
keyPoints: High sodium intake and low potassium intake are linked to increased BP and stroke risk.
Physical inactivity is common, with more than a quarter of U.S. adults reporting no physical activity outside work.
Approximately 75% of U.S. adults are overweight or obese (BMI ≥25 kg/m²).
Alcohol consumption: About 85% of U.S. adults have consumed alcohol at some point.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Lifestyle Factors on Hypertension
items.content: Lifestyle factors such as poor diet, lack of exercise, and excessive alcohol consumption are prevalent and significantly impact blood pressure levels. These factors are common worldwide and serve as targets for interventions aimed at lowering BP.
keyPoints: About 50% of U.S. adults attempt a heart-healthy diet, but this may be an overestimate.
Most adults exceed WHO-recommended sodium intake and do not meet potassium intake levels.
75% of U.S. adults are overweight or obese (BMI ≥25 kg/m²).
Half of U.S. adults engage in less than the recommended 150 minutes/week of aerobic exercise.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.title: Identifying Secondary Hypertension: Key Indicators
items.content: Secondary hypertension should be considered in patients with new-onset hypertension or those whose hypertension is resistant to treatment, worsens abruptly, shows disproportionate target organ damage, or presents with unprovoked hypokalemia, proteinuria, or left ventricular hypertrophy.
keyPoints: Consider secondary causes for new-onset hypertension and prevalent hypertension with specific indicators.

Indicators include: (1) treatment-resistant hypertension; (2) abrupt worsening of hypertension; (3) disproportionate target organ damage; (4) unprovoked hypokalemia, proteinuria, or left ventricular hypertrophy.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea as a Common Cause of Secondary Hypertension
items.content: OSA is the most common cause of secondary hypertension, associated with overweight and obesity. It affects up to 15-30% of adult men and 10-15% of adult women in the U.S., with >50% of those with OSA having hypertension.
keyPoints: OSA prevalence: 15–30% in adult men, 10–15% in adult women.
>50% of adults with OSA have hypertension; >30% of hypertensive adults have OSA.
Common indicators include snoring, poor sleep quality, and daytime sleepiness.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.title: Medications and Substances as Causes of Secondary Hypertension
items.content: Various medications and substances can cause or exacerbate hypertension. Common culprits include amphetamines, NSAIDs, oral contraceptives, and illicit drugs like cocaine.
keyPoints: Common prescription drugs increasing BP: amphetamines, antidepressants, decongestants, oral contraceptives, NSAIDs, corticosteroids.
Illicit substances raising BP include cocaine, marijuana, amphetamines.
>26% of U.S. adults reported regular NSAID use in NHANES 1999–2004.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.title: Renal Parenchymal Disease and Hypertension
items.content: Renal parenchymal disease is a common cause of secondary hypertension, accounting for more than 5% of cases. It can result from various kidney diseases, with glomerular disorders being somewhat more prevalent. The pathophysiology often involves activation of the RAAS, complicating the determination of whether high blood pressure (BP) is a cause or consequence of kidney disease.
keyPoints: Accounts for >5% of non-OSA and medication-related secondary hypertension cases.
More common with glomerular than interstitial disorders.
Activation of RAAS is often involved in the pathophysiology.
Clinical indicators include treatment resistance, fatigue, palpation of a renal mass, hematuria, proteinuria, elevated creatinine, or hyperglycemia.
Imaging (sonography, CT, MRI) and renal biopsy can aid diagnosis.
Control may require three or more antihypertensive agents plus lifestyle management.
Diuretics and RAAS inhibitors are key components of treatment.
Chlorthalidone effective even with serum creatinine >3 mg/dL.
Finerenone enhances BP reduction in advanced CKD.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.title: Primary Aldosteronism as a Cause of Secondary Hypertension
items.content: Primary aldosteronism is responsible for approximately 5% of secondary hypertension cases in adults with non-OSA and medication-related causes. It often presents subtly, but can include resistant hypertension or symptoms related to hyperaldosteronism such as hypokalemia or cardiac arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Causes ~5% of secondary hypertension in adults with non-OSA and medication-related cases.
60–70% have idiopathic bilateral adrenal hyperplasia; 30–40% have a benign adenoma, usually unilateral.
Screening includes abdominal CT and measurement of high aldosterone/renin ratio under standardized conditions.
Adrenal vein aldosterone sampling is the gold standard for diagnosis.
Adrenalectomy can cure or improve hypertension in patients with a unilateral adenoma.
Medical management should include MRA agents like spironolactone, though alternatives may be needed due to side effects.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.title: Diagnostic and Management Strategies for Primary Aldosteronism
items.content: Diagnosis of primary aldosteronism requires careful attention to detail, often involving noninvasive tests like saline suppression or captopril challenge. Adrenal vein sampling is the definitive test. Treatment can involve adrenalectomy by experienced teams for unilateral adenomas, while medical management includes MRAs.
keyPoints: Screening and diagnostic tests require specialized experience.
Adrenal adenomas are small; removal success depends on experienced surgical teams.
Rarely caused by adrenal carcinoma or ectopic malignancy.
Medical management involves MRA agents like spironolactone, with alternatives for side effects.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.title: Recognizing Renovascular Hypertension
items.content: Renovascular hypertension is caused by renal artery obstruction, often due to atherosclerosis or fibromuscular disease. It's crucial to identify the underlying cause as it influences management strategies.
keyPoints: Renovascular obstructions can lead to hypertension and impaired renal function.
Atherosclerotic renovascular disease is unilateral in ~90% of cases, often found near the renal artery origin.
Fibromuscular disease should be suspected in young white women with abrupt-onset or resistant hypertension.
Renal angiography is the gold standard for diagnosing renovascular disease.
Lesions occluding >70% of the vessel lumen are more likely to cause hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.title: Diagnostic Approach in Renovascular Hypertension
items.content: A variety of diagnostic tests can be used to screen for renovascular disease, with renal angiography being definitive. The threshold for investigation is lower when fibromuscular disease is suspected.
keyPoints: Screening tests include renal ultrasound, CT/MRI angiography, and plasma renin activity measurement.
Renal venous renin sampling is rarely used due to high false-positive/negative rates.
Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty can cure or improve hypertension in fibromuscular disease.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.title: Management of Renovascular Hypertension
items.content: Management strategies differ based on the underlying cause. Atherosclerotic renovascular hypertension is managed with medication, while fibromuscular disease may benefit from angioplasty.
keyPoints: Atherosclerotic renovascular hypertension is managed with nonpharmacologic and RAAS-blocking drugs.
Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty shows benefits in fibromuscular disease but not in atherosclerotic cases.
Arteritis or inflammatory diseases should be treated alongside medical management if they cause renovascular hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.title: Management of Hypertension in Pregnancy
items.content: Hypertensive disorders during pregnancy include chronic hypertension, gestational hypertension, preeclampsia potentially culminating in eclampsia, and chronic hypertension with superimposed preeclampsia. Management often involves bed rest, nonpharmacological interventions, and pharmacotherapy.
keyPoints: Progression to eclampsia occurs in <1% of women with mild and about 3% with severe preeclampsia.
Antihypertensive therapy during pregnancy reduces the risk of progression to more severe hypertension compared to placebo.
ACEI and ARB are contraindicated due to teratogenic risks.
Labetalol, nifedipine, and methyldopa are recommended as first-line antihypertensive therapies.
Clinical trials show no benefit for intensive therapy targeting SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg compared to <140/90 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.title: Cushing's Syndrome as a Cause of Secondary Hypertension
items.content: Cushing's syndrome, characterized by excessive cortisol production, is associated with hypertension in more than 75% of patients. It can result from pituitary adenomas, ectopic ACTH-producing tumors, adrenal adenomas, or glucocorticoid treatment.
keyPoints: Clinical signs include moon face, buffalo hump, thin limbs, stretch marks, easy bruising, slow healing, and acne.
Diagnosis involves 24-h urinary corticosteroid levels, imaging tests, and an overnight dexamethasone-suppression test.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.title: Pheochromocytoma as a Cause of Secondary Hypertension
items.content: Pheochromocytoma, caused by excessive catecholamine production from a neuroendocrine tumor, often presents with hypertension at ages 30–50. It can cause paroxysmal attacks with high BP, sweating, headaches, and cardiac arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Screening involves blood and 24-h urinary catecholamine levels and imaging studies like CT, MRI, or PET.
Treatment typically includes complete or partial adrenalectomy using minimally invasive techniques.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.title: Nonpharmacologic Interventions for Elevated BP
items.content: For adults with elevated blood pressure (SBP 120–129 mmHg and DBP <80 mmHg), nonpharmacologic therapy should be actively advised to manage their condition.
keyPoints: Elevated BP defined as SBP 120–129 mmHg and DBP <80 mmHg
Nonpharmacologic interventions are recommended for adults with elevated BP
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.title: Management of Stage 1 Hypertension
items.content: Most adults with stage 1 hypertension (SBP 130–139 mmHg or DBP 80–89 mmHg) should be managed primarily through nonpharmacologic interventions, but those with a history of CVD or high ASCVD risk (~30% in the U.S.) require antihypertensive medication.
keyPoints: Stage 1 hypertension defined as SBP 130–139 mmHg or DBP 80–89 mmHg
~30% of adults with stage 1 hypertension and high ASCVD risk should receive antihypertensive medication
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.title: DASH Diet Efficacy in BP Reduction
items.content: The DASH diet, high in vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and low-fat dairy, is effective for lowering blood pressure. It was specifically formulated to address hypertension.
keyPoints: Reduces SBP/DBP by 5.5/3.0 mmHg compared with the usual U.S. diet in an 8-week feeding trial.
In longer-term behavior change trials, reduces SBP and DBP by ~4 mmHg and 3 mmHg respectively compared to advice-only interventions.
With good adherence, can result in an average SBP reduction of ~5 mmHg in patients with hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.title: Behavior Change Challenges and Success Factors
items.content: Nonpharmacologic interventions often require behavior change, which can be difficult to achieve and maintain. Success is more likely in patients with high CVD risk or those motivated to improve health behaviors.
keyPoints: Behavior change is hard but successful when patients accept the need for it and receive knowledgeable counseling.
Older adults and those with higher SES are generally more successful at behavior change.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.title: DASH Diet Accessibility and Customization
items.content: The DASH diet is accessible through supermarkets without needing specialty foods. It can be customized to fit individual dietary needs, preferences, and lifestyle.
keyPoints: Community-based trials show DASH components are purchasable at regular supermarket locations.
Can be combined with other interventions like weight loss or sodium reduction.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.title: Weight Loss and Its Impact on Blood Pressure
items.content: Weight loss can significantly lower blood pressure, improve lipid levels, and aid in diabetes management. It is achieved through calorie reduction and increased physical activity, with gradual weight loss being most sustainable.
keyPoints: Any weight reduction is beneficial; high-quality counseling typically results in 6- to 12-month weight loss of ~10 lb (4.5 kg).
A 1-mmHg reduction in SBP is expected for every kilogram of weight lost, translating to an average SBP reduction of ~5 mmHg over 6-12 months.
SBP reductions are about 5 mmHg in those with hypertension and 2–3 mmHg in those without.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.title: Dietary Sodium Reduction for Blood Pressure Control
items.content: Reducing dietary sodium intake is effective in lowering blood pressure. A dose-response relationship exists between decreased sodium intake and BP reduction, with significant effects observed across various subgroups.
keyPoints: An SBP/DBP difference of ~15/10 mmHg was noted between the highest (~7000 mg/d) and lowest (<1000 mg/d) sodium intakes.
A 2300-mg (100-mmol) decrement in dietary sodium intake results in an average BP reduction of ~5.5 mmHg.
The effect is greater in adults with hypertension and on SBP compared to DBP.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.title: Guidelines for Sodium Intake
items.content: Current guidelines recommend reducing sodium intake to manage blood pressure, with varying recommendations from different health organizations. Most U.S. adults exceed the recommended thresholds.
keyPoints: U.S. Dietary Guidelines recommend <2300 mg daily; WHO recommends <2000 mg.
More than 95% of U.S. adults exceed the 2000 mg/d threshold, and almost 100% exceed the 1500 mg/d threshold.
A diet with 1500 mg sodium is adequate for meeting other dietary requirements in healthy U.S. adults.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.title: Reducing Dietary Sodium Intake
items.content: Behavior change interventions are effective in reducing dietary sodium intake, primarily by avoiding high-sodium foods and preparing meals at home. In the U.S., over 80% of sodium intake comes from processed foods.
keyPoints: Over 80% of sodium intake is due to food processing or commercial preparation.
Reduction strategies include reading food labels, reducing portion sizes, using low-sodium condiments, and substituting salt with herbs/spices.
Salt substitutes can reduce sodium by ~25%, replacing it with potassium.
A clinical trial showed a 14% reduction in stroke risk with potassium-substituted salt.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.title: Potassium Supplementation and Blood Pressure Reduction
items.content: Increasing dietary potassium intake is associated with lower blood pressure and reduced cardiovascular disease risk. Potassium supplementation, especially through diet, can reduce systolic blood pressure by ~5 mmHg.
keyPoints: Adequate intake levels are 2600 mg/d for women and 3400 mg/d for men.
Potassium supplementation reduces SBP by ~5 mmHg with a net change in urinary potassium ≥20 mmol/day.
SBP reduction is greater (~5 mmHg) in hypertensive adults compared to non-hypertensive (~2 mmHg).
Black adults experience almost three times the SBP lowering effect compared to white adults.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.title: DASH Diet and Potassium Intake
items.content: The DASH diet, which provides ~4700 mg of potassium per day, exceeds the recommended intake for U.S. adults and is beneficial in reducing blood pressure.
keyPoints: Fruits, vegetables, legumes, and leafy greens are high in potassium.
DASH diet targets 4700 mg/day, exceeding the 3500 mg/d recommendation.
Potassium supplementation is contraindicated in hyperkalemia or advanced kidney disease.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.title: Physical Activity's Role in Lowering Blood Pressure
items.content: Increased physical activity, particularly aerobic exercise, is effective in reducing blood pressure (BP) in both hypertensive and non-hypertensive adults. Aerobic exercises like brisk walking, swimming, or dancing have shown substantial BP-lowering efficacy. Meta-analyses indicate reductions of 5–10 mmHg for systolic blood pressure (SBP) in hypertensives and 3–5 mmHg in those without hypertension. Combining aerobic with dynamic resistance exercise can further reduce SBP by approximately 6.5 mmHg.
keyPoints: Aerobic exercise reduces SBP by ~5 mmHg in adults with hypertension.
Combination of aerobic and dynamic resistance exercises lowers SBP by ~6.5 mmHg.
Moderate-intensity aerobic activity for 40–60 minutes, three or more times per week (total ≥150 min/week) is optimal.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.title: Impact of Alcohol Consumption on Blood Pressure
items.content: Alcohol consumption has a linear dose-response relationship with blood pressure and hypertension. Each additional 14 mg of alcohol intake increases SBP by approximately 1.5 mmHg. Reducing alcohol intake through substitution, behavior change interventions, or abstinence can lower SBP by ~5.5 mmHg. U.S. guidelines recommend ≤2 drinks per day for men and ≤1 drink per day for women.
keyPoints: Each 14 mg increase in alcohol raises SBP by ~1.5 mmHg.
Reduction of alcohol intake can decrease SBP by ~5.5 mmHg.
U.S. guidelines suggest ≤2 drinks/day for men and ≤1 drink/day for women.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.title: Combining Nonpharmacologic Interventions for Greater BP Reduction
items.content: Nonpharmacologic interventions exhibit a linear dose-response relationship with blood pressure reduction. Combining multiple interventions, such as the DASH diet with weight loss or sodium reduction, results in greater BP lowering. These combined approaches are more effective in individuals starting with higher abnormalities and can enhance the effects of antihypertensive medications.
keyPoints: Greater BP reduction is achieved by combining interventions like the DASH diet with weight loss or sodium reduction.
Interventions show a linear dose-response for BP reduction.
Combination approaches are more effective in individuals with higher baseline abnormalities.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Efficacy of Antihypertensive Therapy in Reducing Cardiovascular Events
items.content: Antihypertensive drug therapy significantly reduces major cardiovascular events compared to placebo. Historical trials and meta-analyses have demonstrated substantial benefits, particularly when systolic blood pressure (SBP) reductions are targeted.
keyPoints: Meta-analysis of 123 trials showed active treatment resulted in a reduction of major CVD by 20%, CHD by 17%, stroke by 27%, HF by 28%, and all-cause mortality by 13%.
Benefits observed across various baseline SBP levels, notably from 130 to 139 mmHg and above.
Relative risk reduction is similar for individuals with or without prior CVD.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Choice of Antihypertensive Drug Classes
items.content: Among the five primary classes of antihypertensive drugs, diuretics are considered superior for first-step prevention of cardiovascular disease. Beta blockers show inferiority in preventing stroke compared to other drug classes.
keyPoints: Diuretics, beta blockers, CCBs, ACEIs, and ARBs are recommended for initial therapy.
Meta-analyses indicate diuretics as the 'best in class' for first-step prevention of CVD.
Beta blockers are inferior to other classes, particularly for stroke prevention; CCBs are less effective than diuretics for HF prevention.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Guidelines for Managing Stage 1 Hypertension
items.content: Nonpharmacologic therapies are recommended as initial management for adults with stage 1 hypertension who are not at high risk for cardiovascular disease. Pharmacologic therapy is considered if blood pressure targets are unmet after six months.
keyPoints: Use nonpharmacologic therapy initially in stage 1 hypertension without high CVD risk (10-year ASCVD risk <10%).
Consider adding antihypertensive medication if BP remains above SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg after six months of nonpharmacologic therapy.
Pharmacologic therapy is advised for those with stage 1 hypertension at high CVD risk and all individuals with stage 2 hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Preferred Antihypertensive Agents for Comorbid Conditions
items.content: In patients with hypertension and comorbid conditions like diabetes mellitus, CHD, or CKD, longer-acting antihypertensive agents are recommended to ensure effective once-daily treatment. Longer-acting diuretics such as chlorthalidone or indapamide are preferred over shorter-acting ones due to their extended half-life and proven efficacy in landmark trials.
keyPoints: Longer-acting diuretics like chlorthalidone or indapamide are preferred for nighttime and daytime BP control.
Chlorthalidone has been used in almost all landmark U.S. trials of antihypertensive drug treatment.
Thiazide and thiazide-like diuretics can cause mild biochemical changes, such as hypokalemia and slight increases in blood glucose.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: ACEIs vs. ARBs: Usage and Side Effects
items.content: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are similar in clinical effects but differ in side effect profiles. ACEIs can cause a dry cough, which is often resolved by switching to an ARB. Both should not be used together due to increased risk of hyperkalemia without additional benefits.
keyPoints: ACEIs like lisinopril are preferred for their longer-acting properties.
Common side effect of ACEIs is a dry cough, which can be resolved by switching to an ARB.
Combining ACEIs and ARBs increases the risk of hyperkalemia without additional benefits.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: CCB Use in Hypertension: Considerations and Side Effects
items.content: Dihydropyridine CCBs are commonly used for hypertension but can cause peripheral edema (~10% of patients). Nondihydropyridines should be reserved for specific indications due to risks like bradycardia, heart block, or HF.
keyPoints: Peripheral edema occurs in ~10% of patients taking dihydropyridine CCBs.
Dihydropyridine CCBs are primarily vasoactive and commonly used for hypertension.
Nondihydropyridine CCBs should be used cautiously, especially in patients at risk for bradycardia or heart block.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Optimal Blood Pressure Targets for CVD Prevention
items.content: Meta-analyses and clinical trials suggest that lower achieved blood pressure (BP) levels are associated with greater cardiovascular disease (CVD) protection, especially in individuals starting at higher BP levels. The ACC/AHA guideline recommends an SBP/DBP target of <130/80 mmHg for adults with hypertension, except for noninstitutionalized older adults ≥65 years where the goal is to achieve an SBP <130 mmHg.
keyPoints: Lower achieved BP levels are associated with greater CVD protection.
Achieved SBP of <130 mmHg is superior to higher categories for CVD prevention.
Recommended SBP/DBP target: <130/80 mmHg for adults with hypertension.
For noninstitutionalized older adults ≥65 years, the goal should be an SBP <130 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Management of Resistant Hypertension
items.content: Resistant hypertension is defined as failure to control SBP/DBP to <130/80 mmHg with three antihypertensive medications, including a diuretic, or requiring four or more medications. Management includes confirming adherence, using long-acting agents, and considering mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRAs) like spironolactone, eplerenone, or finerenone.
keyPoints: Resistant hypertension: SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg not achieved with three medications including a diuretic, or four or more medications.
Confirm adherence to treatment and lifestyle changes.
Use long-acting, once-daily antihypertensive agents.
Consider adding MRAs like spironolactone, eplerenone, or finerenone.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Renal Denervation Therapy for Resistant Hypertension
items.content: For patients with true resistant hypertension and an office SBP ≥160 mmHg, renal nerve denervation (RDN) can be considered as an adjunctive treatment. This approach is supported by the 2023 FDA approval of ultrasound and radiofrequency renal denervation therapy.
keyPoints: Consider RDN for patients with true resistant hypertension and office SBP ≥160 mmHg.
FDA approved ultrasound and radiofrequency renal denervation therapy in 2023.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: Antihypertensive Strategies for Patients with CVD
items.content: In patients with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease (CVD), the choice of antihypertensive agents is influenced by the specific type of CVD. Commonly used medications include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, beta blockers, often combined with diuretics, calcium channel blockers (CCB), and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRA). Additional treatment with SGLT2 inhibitors may be beneficial, particularly for those with heart failure and diabetes mellitus.
keyPoints: ACEI, ARB, beta blockers are commonly used in CVD patients
Diuretics, CCB, MRA often accompany primary agents
SGLT2 inhibitors indicated especially in HF and diabetes
Standard treatment goal: SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg if well tolerated
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: Managing Hypertension in CKD
items.content: Hypertension is prevalent among patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), especially as the severity increases. The management of hypertension in these patients involves multiple antihypertensive agents and nonpharmacologic measures like sodium reduction. RAAS blockers such as ACEIs and ARBs are crucial due to their ability to preserve glomerular function by reducing intraglomerular pressure, particularly important for those with proteinuria.
keyPoints: 80-85% of advanced kidney disease patients have hypertension
RAAS blockers (ACEI, ARB) central in preserving glomerular function
Diuretics reduce peripheral resistance and volume overload
Chlorthalidone shows BP reduction by 10.5 mmHg and halves urinary albumin/creatinine ratio
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: Risks of Dual RAAS Blockade in Kidney Disease
items.content: Dual therapy with ACEIs, ARBs, or their combination is risky for kidney disease patients due to the potential for hyperkalemia and further decline in kidney function. Careful monitoring of electrolyte levels, particularly serum potassium, is essential when using potassium-sparing agents like ACEI, ARB, and MRA drugs.
keyPoints: Dual RAAS blockade (ACEI/ARB) increases risk of hyperkalemia
Can lead to further decline in kidney function
Monitor electrolytes, especially serum potassium levels
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.title: Antihypertensive Targets and Therapy in Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: In patients with diabetes mellitus, achieving an SBP/DBP target of <130/90 mmHg is crucial to reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease. Due to common comorbidities like kidney insufficiency or heavy proteinuria, combination therapy including ACEIs or ARBs and diuretics is often required.
keyPoints: Antihypertensive target: SBP/DBP <130/90 mmHg
Combination therapy usually needed; many require three or four agents
ACEI or ARB recommended, especially with proteinuria/kidney insufficiency
Diuretics are commonly required
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.title: Managing Hypertension in Older Adults
items.content: Despite challenges like comorbidities and potential drug interactions, older adults benefit from antihypertensive therapy. Treatment should aim for an SBP <130 mmHg with careful monitoring to avoid excessive BP reduction that could lead to syncope or falls.
keyPoints: Initiate treatment with lower doses compared to younger patients
Use diuretics over CCBs in dual-therapy combinations with ACEIs/ARBs when possible
SBP target: <130 mmHg
Intensive therapy reduces risk of cognitive impairment and dementia
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.title: Considerations for Resistant Hypertension in Diabetes
items.content: In patients with diabetes and resistant hypertension, adding an MRA can be effective. However, careful monitoring of serum potassium is necessary due to the risk of hyperkalemia.
keyPoints: Addition of MRA may be effective for resistant hypertension
Monitor serum potassium closely
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 6)
items.title: Racial Disparities in Hypertension Management
items.content: Non-Hispanic blacks have higher BP levels and prevalence of hypertension compared to other racial/ethnic groups, with earlier onset and greater risk for CVD and kidney disease. Despite high awareness and treatment rates, control rates are lower than whites (37% vs 52% at <140/90 mmHg; 20% vs 26% at <130/80 mmHg). Structural changes in healthcare provision and addressing social determinants of health are crucial for improving BP control.
keyPoints: Non-Hispanic blacks have disproportionately high BP levels and prevalence of hypertension.
Higher risk of CVD and kidney disease compared to other racial/ethnic groups.
BP control rates: 37% vs 52% at <140/90 mmHg, 20% vs 26% at <130/80 mmHg in non-Hispanic blacks vs whites (2017–2020 NHANES).
Social determinants of health contribute significantly to disparities.
Diuretics and CCBs are particularly effective for BP control in U.S. blacks.
Structural changes needed: patient engagement, team-based care, high-quality healthcare policies.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 6)
items.title: Sex Differences in Hypertension Management
items.content: Women generally have better hypertension awareness, treatment rates, and BP control compared to men. ACEIs and ARBs are contraindicated during pregnancy or for women planning pregnancy. Thiazide diuretics may reduce fracture risk due to decreased urinary calcium excretion, while loop diuretics do not show this association. Women experience more cough with ACEIs and pedal edema with CCBs.
keyPoints: Women have better hypertension awareness and BP control than men, especially at younger ages.
ACEIs and ARBs contraindicated in pregnancy or for women planning pregnancy.
Thiazide diuretics may reduce fracture risk via decreased urinary calcium excretion.
Loop diuretics do not show association with fractures or decreased bone marrow density.
Women report more cough with ACEIs and pedal edema with CCBs.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 7)
items.title: Management of Hypertensive Urgencies vs. Emergencies
items.content: Hypertensive urgencies and emergencies are differentiated by the presence of symptoms indicating end-organ damage. Urgencies, defined as BP ≥180/100 mmHg without symptoms, can be managed with oral antihypertensives in an outpatient setting. Emergencies require intravenous therapy due to active organ damage.
keyPoints: BP threshold for urgency/emergency: SBP/DBP ≥ 180/100 mmHg
Urgency management: Oral antihypertensives, outpatient care
Emergency management: Intravenous agents, inpatient or ER setting
Common IV agents: Sodium nitroprusside, nicardipine, labetalol
Nicardipine contraindicated in severe aortic stenosis
Labetalol contraindicated in obstructive airways diseases, bradycardia, or heart block
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 7)
items.title: Approach to BP Reduction in Hypertensive Emergencies
items.content: In hypertensive emergencies, rapid but controlled reduction of BP is crucial. Intravenous agents like sodium nitroprusside and nicardipine are preferred for their efficacy. Avoid overshooting the target BP to prevent hypotension. Secondary causes such as renovascular disease should be evaluated post-stabilization.
keyPoints: Goal: Reduction, not immediate normalization of BP
Preferred IV agents: Sodium nitroprusside (requires careful monitoring), nicardipine
Avoid overshooting target BP to prevent hypotension
Evaluate secondary causes like renovascular disease after stabilization
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE, AWARENESS, TREATMENT, AND CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Understanding Hypertension Prevalence in the U.S.
items.content: The NHANES data provides consistent estimates of hypertension prevalence using specific SBP/DBP cut points. These estimates show stable prevalence rates over time but highlight disparities across age, race/ethnicity, and gender.
keyPoints: NHANES uses BP measurements from a single visit, potentially overestimating hypertension.
Stable prevalence estimates: ~46% for SBP/DBP ≥ 130/80 mmHg; ~32% for SBP/DBP ≥ 140/90 mmHg.
Higher prevalence in older adults, non-Hispanic blacks, and men compared to younger adults, other race/ethnicity groups, and women.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE, AWARENESS, TREATMENT, AND CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE, AWARENESS, TREATMENT, AND CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Trends in Hypertension Control Rates in the U.S.
items.content: Hypertension control rates improved until 2009–2012 but declined thereafter. There are significant disparities in control rates between non-Hispanic blacks and other race/ethnicity groups, with older adults experiencing a more pronounced decline.
keyPoints: Control rates increased to ~53% for SBP/DBP <140/90 mmHg by 2009–2012, then declined to ~48% by 2017–2020.
Control rates for SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg were ~26% in 2009–2012 and decreased to ~24% by 2017–2020.
Non-Hispanic blacks had lower control rates (~37%) compared to whites (~52%) for SBP/DBP <140/90 mmHg in 2017–2020.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE, AWARENESS, TREATMENT, AND CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: IMPROVING THE CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Team-Based Care Enhances Hypertension Control
items.content: Team-based care significantly improves hypertension control by involving a coordinated team of healthcare professionals. This model allows nonphysician professionals to prescribe antihypertensive medications under physician oversight, optimizing time for physicians to manage complex cases.
keyPoints: Team-based care has shown the biggest improvement in RCTs and meta-analyses.
Average SBP reduction with team-based care is ~7 mmHg; a high-quality RCT showed an exceedance of 23 mmHg.
Nonphysician professionals can prescribe antihypertensive medications, freeing physician time for complex cases.
items.reference: Hypertension · IMPROVING THE CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: IMPROVING THE CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION
items.title: Home Blood Pressure Monitoring (HBPM) in Hypertension Management
items.content: HBPM engages patients in their care and complements office BP management. Advising on the purchase of valid devices and training for accurate measurement is crucial. A recommended strategy involves three morning and evening HBPM measurements over 3 days before a clinic visit.
keyPoints: HBPM provides an excellent way to engage patients in their own care.
Recommended: Three morning and three evening HBPM measurements for 3 days prior to a clinic visit.
Use of HBPM complements office BP management (OBPM) for long-term hypertension control.
items.reference: Hypertension · IMPROVING THE CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: Which system is primarily responsible for the long-term regulation of arterial blood pressure according to Arthur Guyton's hypothesis?
items.explanation: Arthur Guyton hypothesized that the kidney's ability to excrete sodium ultimately dictates long-term changes in blood pressure.
items.reference: Hypertension · Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: What is a primary role of endothelial cells in the regulation of vascular tone?
options: Synthesizing and releasing relaxing and contracting factors
items.explanation: Endothelial cells regulate vascular tone by synthesizing and releasing relaxing and contracting factors.
items.reference: Hypertension · Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: What is the initial response in arterial pressure due to high intake of sodium chloride?
items.explanation: An initial response to a high intake of sodium chloride is an increase in arterial pressure, which may involve increased glomerular filtration rate.
items.reference: Hypertension · Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: Which mechanism helps maintain sodium balance at the expense of an increase in arterial pressure?
items.explanation: The pressure-natriuresis mechanism helps maintain sodium balance by increasing urinary sodium excretion at the expense of increased arterial pressure.
items.reference: Hypertension · Hypertension | BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.question: Which class of drugs works by antagonizing the neurotransmitter acetylcholine in addition to epinephrine and norepinephrine?
items.explanation: Beta blockers can antagonize the neurotransmitter acetylcholine as well as epinephrine and norepinephrine.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.question: Which drug class is primarily used for BP control due to its effect on reducing peripheral arteriolar resistance over the long term?
items.explanation: Thiazide and thiazide-like diuretics work by reducing peripheral arteriolar resistance over the long term.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)
items.question: Which drug class is rarely used for BP control due to side effects and diminishing efficacy over time?
items.explanation: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors are rarely used for BP control due to side effects and diminishing efficacy over time.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE PHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended practice for obtaining accurate office BP measurements?
options: Instructing the patient to avoid caffeine, smoking, alcohol, or exercise for 30 min prior to the measurements.
Using a quiet room with a comfortable ambient temperature.
Having the patient rest for only 1 minute before taking the first measurement.
Seating the patient in a chair with upright back support and feet flat on the ground.
items.explanation: Patients should rest for 3–5 minutes prior to the first measurement, not just 1 minute.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.question: What is recommended when choosing a BP measurement device?
options: Selecting any available device without validation.
Using a clinically validated, preferably automated BP measurement device.
Choosing a cuff size based on the patient's height rather than arm circumference.
Opting for manual devices to avoid calibration issues.
items.explanation: A clinically validated, preferably automated BP measurement device is recommended to minimize errors and eliminate recalibration needs.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.question: What should be done if the patient's BP varies randomly within and between visits?
options: Rely on a single reading for classification.
Use an average of repeated readings obtained at more than one visit.
Ignore variations as they are clinically insignificant.
Only consider readings from the first visit.
items.explanation: An average of repeated readings obtained at more than one visit is recommended to account for random BP variation.
items.reference: Hypertension · BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION
items.question: According to the ACC/AHA classification, what systolic BP (SBP) or diastolic BP (DBP) reading is used as a threshold for defining hypertension in adults?
items.explanation: The ACC/AHA classification defines hypertension as an SBP or DBP ≥130 or 80 mmHg, respectively.
items.reference: Hypertension · DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION
items.question: When systolic and diastolic BP readings fall into different categories, how should the higher category be determined?
items.explanation: When SBP and DBP are in different BP categories, the higher classification should be chosen.
items.reference: Hypertension · DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION
items.question: What is a common error in routine clinical practice regarding systolic blood pressure (SBP) measurement?
items.explanation: Generally, SBP is overestimated by an average of ~7 mmHg in routine clinical practice.
items.reference: Hypertension · DEFINITION OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.question: Which condition is characterized by office blood pressures meeting criteria for hypertension while out-of-office readings do not?
items.explanation: White coat hypertension is when office BPs meet criteria for hypertension but out-of-office readings are nonhypertensive.
items.reference: Hypertension · OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.question: What is the recommended approach to obtaining home blood pressure measurements?
options: Use a clinically validated device and average 2-3 readings in the morning and evening.
Measure BP once daily at any time convenient for the patient.
Only measure BP after taking antihypertensive medications.
Use non-clinically validated devices to ensure comfort.
items.explanation: Patients should use a clinically validated device and average 2-3 readings in the morning (before medication) and evening.
items.reference: Hypertension · OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.question: What is the significance of nighttime blood pressure measurements?
options: They are irrelevant for cardiovascular risk assessment.
They provide the best prediction of CVD risk, especially in nondippers.
They should be avoided as they cause discomfort.
They are only useful if daytime readings are normal.
items.explanation: Nighttime BP measurements can predict CVD risk well, particularly for those who do not show the usual nighttime BP decrease (nondippers).
items.reference: Hypertension · OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT
items.question: Which group is more likely to exhibit BP nondipping?
items.explanation: BP nondipping is more common in non-Hispanic blacks compared to other major racial/ethnic groups.
items.reference: Hypertension · OUT-OF-OFFICE BLOOD PRESSURE MEASUREMENT

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION
items.question: What is the prevalence of hypertension among U.S. adults aged 75 years or older?
items.explanation: The prevalence increases to 80–85% in those aged 75 years or older.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION
items.question: Which racial/ethnic group in the United States has the highest reported prevalence of hypertension?
items.explanation: Non-Hispanic black adults have a prevalence of hypertension (59%) that is substantially higher compared to other groups.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION
items.question: What factor does NOT contribute to the progression of age-related increases in blood pressure among isolated populations when they migrate?
items.explanation: Increases, not decreases, in potassium intake are associated with progression to age-related increases in BP.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION
items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of hypertension in low- and middle-income countries using SBP ≥140 mmHg, DBP ≥90 mmHg, or taking antihypertensive medication?
items.explanation: The prevalence is estimated to be 31.5% in low- and middle-income countries.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for conducting basic laboratory testing in patients with newly diagnosed hypertension?
options: Recognize the presence and extent of target organ damage.
Facilitate the identification of secondary causes of hypertension, including kidney disease and primary aldosteronism.
Estimate atherosclerotic CVD risk in patients without a history of CVD.
Determine the patient's genetic predisposition to hypertension.
items.explanation: Basic laboratory testing is not used to determine genetic predisposition to hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK

items.subtopic: LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK
items.question: Which test is recommended as part of the basic laboratory evaluation for newly diagnosed hypertension?
items.explanation: Serum creatinine and estimated GFR are part of the recommended basic laboratory evaluation.
items.reference: Hypertension · LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK

items.subtopic: LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK
items.question: What is a key factor in estimating ASCVD risk for U.S. adults aged 40–75 years using the ACC/AHA pooled cohort equations?
options: It has been validated in non-Hispanic white and black adults.
It incorporates social determinants of health.
It is used for patients with a history of prior CVD.
items.explanation: The ACC/AHA pooled cohort equations have been validated in non-Hispanic white and black adults.
items.reference: Hypertension · LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK

items.subtopic: LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK
items.question: Which of the following is a feature of the PREVENT risk predicting model?
options: It can only predict 10-year CVD risk.
It excludes assessment of kidney function.
It includes prediction of heart failure risk.
It does not use hemoglobin A1c.
items.explanation: The PREVENT model includes the prediction of heart failure risk.
items.reference: Hypertension · LABORATORY AND OTHER INVESTIGATIONS | ESTIMATION OF CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE RISK

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: Which factor has been identified as a risk factor for stroke in many studies?
items.explanation: Insufficient dietary potassium intake has been identified as a risk factor for stroke.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: What is the typical increase in systolic blood pressure (SBP) associated with exposure to air pollution?
items.explanation: The increase in SBP due to air pollution is typically ~3–5 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: Which of the following exposures is NOT one of the six personal exposures strongly associated with higher BP and age-related increases in BP?
items.explanation: High altitude exposure is not listed as one of the six personal exposures.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: Which environmental factor's impact on BP varies by geography, climate, season, and economic development?
items.explanation: The level of exposure to air pollution varies by geography, climate, season, and economic development.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical indicator of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA)?
items.explanation: Snoring is a common clinical indicator of OSA.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of U.S. adults with obstructive sleep apnea have hypertension?
items.explanation: More than half of U.S. adults with OSA have hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: Which treatment for obstructive sleep apnea has been shown to have limited blood pressure lowering effects?
items.explanation: Meta-analyses demonstrate limited BP lowering due to CPAP treatment.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following substances is NOT mentioned as a cause of new-onset hypertension?
items.explanation: Aspirin is not mentioned as a cause of new-onset hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical indicator of hypertension due to renal parenchymal disease?
items.explanation: Clinical indicators include treatment resistance, fatigue, palpation of a renal mass, hematuria, proteinuria, elevated creatinine, or hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.question: What is often part of the underlying pathophysiology that leads to hypertension in renal parenchymal disease?
items.explanation: The activation of the RAAS is often a part of the underlying pathophysiology that leads to hypertension in renal parenchymal disease.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.question: Which class of drugs is traditionally considered ineffective for managing patients with serum creatinine >3 mg/dL but has shown efficacy in a recent trial?
items.explanation: Traditionally, thiazide and thiazide-like diuretics were thought to be relatively ineffective for patients with serum creatinine >3 mg/dL, but a recent trial showed efficacy with chlorthalidone.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.question: What is the gold standard test for diagnosing primary aldosteronism?
items.explanation: Adrenal vein aldosterone sampling is generally accepted as the gold standard test for diagnosing primary aldosteronism.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with unilateral renal artery obstruction in patients with renovascular hypertension?
items.explanation: Renovascular hypertension due to atherosclerosis is most commonly unilateral with the principal obstruction near the renal artery origin.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: In which demographic group is fibromuscular disease more likely to be suspected as a cause of renovascular hypertension?
options: Patients with bilateral renal artery disease
Patients with abrupt-onset hypertension and resistant hypertension
items.explanation: Fibromuscular disease is more likely in younger, mostly white women.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: What diagnostic test is considered the gold standard for confirming renovascular disease?
options: CT and magnetic resonance angiography imaging studies
items.explanation: Renal angiography is the gold standard diagnostic test for renovascular disease.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: Which treatment has been shown to cure or substantially improve hypertension in patients with fibromuscular disease?
items.explanation: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty can cure or substantially improve hypertension in patients with fibromuscular disease.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: Which antihypertensive medications are recommended as safe first-line therapy during pregnancy?
items.explanation: Labetalol, nifedipine, and methyldopa are recommended as safe first-line antihypertensive drug therapy during pregnancy.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: What percentage of patients with Cushing's syndrome have hypertension?
items.explanation: More than 75% of patients with Cushing's syndrome have hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: Which diagnostic test is used for screening pheochromocytoma?
options: CT scan of the abdomen only
items.explanation: Screening for pheochromocytoma is usually based on blood and 24-h urinary catecholamine values.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: What is the most common congenital cause of hypertension?
items.explanation: Coarctation of the aorta is the most common congenital cause of hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: Which blood pressure range is considered elevated in adults according to the passage?
items.explanation: The passage defines elevated BP as SBP 120–129 mmHg and DBP <80 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: In which group of adults is antihypertensive medication recommended in addition to nonpharmacologic interventions?
options: Adults with a history of CVD or high risk for ASCVD and stage 1 hypertension
items.explanation: Antihypertensive medication is recommended in addition to nonpharmacologic interventions for the minority (~30% in the United States) of adults with stage 1 hypertension who have a history of CVD or are at high risk for ASCVD.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.question: What is not recommended by major clinical practice guidelines according to the passage?
options: Antihypertensive drug therapy for stage 2 hypertension
Low-dose pharmacotherapy for BP lowering in nonhypertensive patients
Combination of nonpharmacologic and antihypertensive drug therapy for stage 2 hypertension
items.explanation: The passage states that drug therapy is not recommended by major clinical practice guidelines for the purpose of BP lowering, prevention of hypertension, regression of left ventricular mass, or prevention of left ventricular hypertrophy in nonhypertensive patients.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: Which nonpharmacologic intervention is specifically formulated to lower blood pressure?
items.explanation: The DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet is specifically formulated to lower blood pressure.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: What was the average reduction in systolic blood pressure (SBP) observed in an 8-week feeding trial of the DASH diet compared to the usual U.S. diet?
items.explanation: The original short-term (8-week) feeding trial of the DASH diet reduced SBP by 5.5 mmHg compared with the usual U.S. diet.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: Which factor is NOT primarily associated with achieving behavior change in nonpharmacologic interventions?
options: Pill supplementation to enhance potassium intake
items.explanation: High-quality counseling programs are associated with achieving behavior change, unlike pill supplementation, renal denervation therapy, and baroreceptor activation therapy.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.question: Which group is more likely to be successful in behavior change interventions for hypertension?
items.explanation: Older adults and those with higher socioeconomic status (SES) tend to be more successful in behavior change interventions.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
