items.question: Which of the following best describes the expected reduction in systolic blood pressure (SBP) for a patient who loses approximately 10 lb (~4.5 kg)?
items.explanation: A 1-mmHg reduction in SBP is expected for every kilogram of weight loss, so ~4.5 kg would result in a ~5 mmHg reduction.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.question: What is the approximate difference in SBP/DBP between individuals with the highest and lowest dietary sodium intake according to a large dose-response meta-analysis?
items.explanation: An SBP/DBP difference of ~15/10 mmHg was noted between those with the highest (~7000 mg/d) and lowest (<1000 mg/d) intakes of sodium.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.question: Which FDA-approved medication is mentioned as an equivalent agent for weight loss in the passage?
items.explanation: Injectable glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists are FDA-approved for weight loss.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.question: What is the recommended daily sodium intake according to the U.S. federal Dietary Guidelines for Americans?
items.explanation: The U.S. federal Dietary Guidelines recommend a daily sodium intake of <2300 mg.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: Which strategy is NOT mentioned as a way to reduce dietary sodium intake?
options: Using salt substitutes that replace ~25% of the sodium content with potassium
Increasing consumption of processed commercial food products
Choosing condiments and seasonings with low sodium content
items.explanation: The passage suggests avoiding high-sodium foods, using salt substitutes, and choosing low-sodium condiments as strategies to reduce dietary sodium intake.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: What was the primary outcome of the large cluster-designed clinical trial conducted in Chinese adults?
options: A significant increase in hyperkalemic events
An impressive and statistically significant 14% reduction in stroke
No change in major cardiovascular disease (CVD) events
items.explanation: The trial demonstrated a 14% reduction in stroke, other major CVD events, and all-cause mortality.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: What is the recommended potassium intake level for men according to U.S. Dietary Reference Intake committees?
items.explanation: The adequate intake level for potassium in men is identified as 3400 mg/d.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.question: In which population was the systolic blood pressure (SBP) reduction greater when supplemented with potassium?
items.explanation: The SBP reduction was almost three times greater in black compared to white adults.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: What is the average reduction in systolic blood pressure (SBP) for adults with hypertension when engaging in aerobic exercise according to meta-analyses?
items.explanation: Meta-analyses report an average reduction of 5–10 mmHg for SBP in adults with hypertension who engage in aerobic exercise.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: Which type of resistance exercise involves tightening muscles without visible movement of surrounding joints?
items.explanation: Isometric resistance exercises involve muscle tightening without visible joint movement.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: According to the passage, what is the recommended frequency and duration of aerobic exercise for optimal BP reduction?
options: 40–60 min three or more times per week
items.explanation: The recommended aerobic exercise duration is 40–60 minutes, three or more times per week.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.question: What effect does reducing alcohol intake have on systolic blood pressure according to the largest meta-analysis?
items.explanation: Reducing alcohol intake leads to a reduction in SBP by approximately 5.5 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which antihypertensive drug class has been shown to be inferior in head-to-head RCTs for the prevention of stroke?
items.explanation: Beta blockers have been inferior to agents from other antihypertensive drug classes, especially for prevention of stroke.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which class of drugs is generally identified as the 'best in class' for first-step prevention of CVD?
items.explanation: Meta-analyses have identified diuretics as being the 'best in class' for first-step prevention of CVD.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: According to clinical practice guidelines, which combination is recommended for initial drug therapy in patients without a compelling indication for use of a beta blocker?
options: Calcium channel blockers (CCBs) and ACE inhibitors (ACEIs)
Diuretics, CCBs, and ACEIs or ARBs
items.explanation: Clinical practice guidelines recommend diuretics, CCBs, and ACEIs or ARBs for initial drug therapy.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: In adults with stage 1 hypertension who are not at high risk for CVD, what is recommended if BP cannot be controlled to an SBP/DBP <130/80 mmHg after 6 months?
options: Increase the dosage of current medications
items.explanation: If BP cannot be controlled to <130/80 mmHg after 6 months, it may be reasonable to add an antihypertensive medication.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: Which longer-acting diuretic is preferred over hydrochlorothiazide for nighttime and daytime control of blood pressure?
items.explanation: Chlorthalidone is preferred due to its long half-life and use in landmark trials.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the most common side effect of ACE inhibitors that can be resolved by switching to an ARB?
items.explanation: A dry cough is the most common side effect of ACE inhibitors, resolved by switching to an ARB.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: Why should ACEIs and ARBs not be used in combination?
options: They increase the risk of hyperkalemia without additional benefit.
They are ineffective for blood pressure control.
items.explanation: Combining ACEIs and ARBs increases the risk of hyperkalemia without additional benefit.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: Which class of beta blockers is not recommended for hypertension due to less cardioprotection?
items.explanation: Atenolol is not recommended for hypertension due to less cardioprotection compared to other drug classes.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: Which systolic blood pressure target is generally recommended for noninstitutionalized community-dwelling older adults, defined as ≥65 years?
items.explanation: An SBP goal of <130 mmHg is recommended for noninstitutionalized community-dwelling older adults.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: What is the treatment goal of SBP/DBP in adults with hypertension according to the ACC/AHA BP guideline?
items.explanation: The recommended SBP/DBP treatment goal is <130/80 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: Which medication should be considered for addition to the regimen in managing resistant hypertension?
items.explanation: An MRA such as spironolactone should be added to the regimen for resistant hypertension.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: What is a common cause of apparent resistant hypertension that should first be excluded?
options: Pseudo-resistance due to inaccurate BP measurement
Lack of adherence to lifestyle changes
Use of medications that raise BP
items.explanation: First, pseudo-resistance due to inaccurate BP measurement should be excluded.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: Which antihypertensive agents are central in preserving glomerular function by inducing efferent arteriolar dilatation and reducing intraglomerular pressure, especially important for patients with proteinuria and advanced kidney disease?
items.explanation: ACEIs and ARBs are central in preserving glomerular function by inducing efferent arteriolar dilatation.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: In patients with an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of approximately 30 mL/min per 1.73 m^2 or lower, which type of diuretic is traditionally recommended?
items.explanation: Loop diuretics are traditionally recommended for patients with an eGFR of ~30 mL/min per 1.73 m^2 or lower.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: Which combination is considered dangerous due to the risk of hyperkalemia and decline in kidney function?
items.explanation: Dual therapy with an ACEI, ARB, or ACEI-ARB combination is dangerous due to the risk of hyperkalemia and decline in kidney function.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: Which antihypertensive agents are particularly useful for patients with kidney disease due to their ability to reduce peripheral resistance and intravascular volume?
items.explanation: Diuretics are particularly useful in reducing both peripheral resistance and intravascular volume.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: In patients with longstanding severe diabetes mellitus, what is a common complication that makes hypertension management difficult?
items.explanation: Autonomic neuropathy or volume depletion can cause orthostatic hypotension in patients with longstanding severe diabetes mellitus.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: What is the recommended antihypertensive treatment target for SBP/DBP in patients with diabetes mellitus?
items.explanation: The recommended antihypertensive treatment target for patients with diabetes mellitus is an SBP/DBP <130/90 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: Which class of drugs should be included in antihypertensive combination therapy for patients with diabetes mellitus and heavy proteinuria?
items.explanation: Inclusion of an ACEI or ARB is recommended in patients with diabetes mellitus and heavy proteinuria.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: What is a common effect of SGLT2 inhibitors on blood pressure in patients with diabetes mellitus?
options: Significant reduction meeting FDA requirements for antihypertensive classification
Modest reduction not meeting FDA requirements for antihypertensive classification
items.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors result in a modest SBP/DBP reduction of ~5/2 mmHg, which does not meet the FDA requirement for classification as an antihypertensive agent.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 6)
items.question: Which antihypertensive medication classes are noted as especially effective for lowering blood pressure in U.S. blacks?
items.explanation: Diuretics and calcium channel blockers (CCBs) are especially effective for lowering blood pressure in U.S. blacks.

items.assertion: Most adults have primary hypertension with no obvious underlying anatomic cause.
items.reason: Primary hypertension is often due to a combination of genetic and environmental factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain the absence of an anatomic cause.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.assertion: High altitude exposure can lead to higher blood pressure.
items.reason: This is due to a combination of hypoxia and cold temperatures at high altitudes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.assertion: The severity of obstructive sleep apnea is directly associated with an individual's level of blood pressure.
items.reason: OSA and hypertension share common pathophysiologic features, including sympathetic system activation due to intermittent hypoxia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the association.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.assertion: Herbal treatments like ginseng and guarana lower blood pressure.
items.reason: These herbal treatments have been reported to raise BP.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these herbs are reported to raise, not lower, BP.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.assertion: Improvement of cardiovascular risk factors is essential in treating patients with chronic kidney disease.
items.reason: Most patients with CKD have a variety of both conventional and novel risk factors for ASCVD placing them at high risk for CVD complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why improvement of cardiovascular risk factors is essential.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.assertion: Finerenone is a thiazide diuretic used in managing hypertension in CKD.
items.reason: Finerenone enhances BP reduction in patients with advanced CKD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because finerenone is a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA), not a thiazide diuretic, although the reason is true.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.assertion: Fibromuscular disease should be suspected in young white women with abrupt-onset hypertension.
items.reason: These patients tend to have diffuse, irregular lesions more commonly found in fibromuscular disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.assertion: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty is beneficial for all patients with renovascular hypertension.
items.reason: It has been proven to control blood pressure and preserve renal function in atherosclerotic renovascular hypertension.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the benefit of angioplasty is mainly seen in fibromuscular disease, not atherosclerotic.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.assertion: Labetalol and nifedipine may be superior to methyldopa for prevention of preeclampsia.
items.reason: One trial suggests that labetalol and nifedipine are more effective than methyldopa in preventing preeclampsia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.assertion: Pheochromocytoma is most commonly diagnosed in individuals over 60 years old.
items.reason: Most patients with pheochromocytoma present with hypertension at a relatively young age, often between 30 and 50 years.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because pheochromocytoma is most commonly diagnosed in younger individuals, not over 60.
items.reference: Hypertension · SECONDARY HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION
items.assertion: Nonpharmacologic interventions are the primary treatment for adults with elevated BP.
items.reason: The passage advises active nonpharmacologic therapy in adults with an elevated BP (SBP 120–129 mmHg and DBP <80 mmHg).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVENTION AND TREATMENT OF PRIMARY HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.assertion: The DASH diet can be combined with other nonpharmacologic interventions.
items.reason: The DASH diet eating plan can be customized to meet an individual's dietary requirements, meal preferences, and lifestyle.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the DASH diet can be combined with other interventions.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)
items.assertion: Feeding studies of the DASH diet are more generalizable to clinical practice than behavior change trials.
items.reason: Feeding studies require less effort by the patient and achieve greater intervention change.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because feeding study results are less generalizable to clinical practice, despite requiring less effort by patients.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.assertion: Weight loss can lead to a reduction in systolic blood pressure.
items.reason: A 1-mmHg reduction in SBP is expected for every kilogram of weight loss.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)
items.assertion: The World Health Organization recommends a daily sodium intake of <2300 mg.
items.reason: The U.S. federal Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommend the same threshold.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; WHO recommends <2000 mg, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.assertion: Potassium supplementation is contraindicated in patients taking potassium-sparing diuretics.
items.reason: These medications can block the urinary excretion of potassium, increasing the risk of hyperkalemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)
items.assertion: The primary outcome of potassium supplementation trials is a reduction in diastolic blood pressure.
items.reason: Potassium has well-known natriuretic effects that contribute to lowering blood pressure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the primary outcome discussed is a reduction in systolic blood pressure, not diastolic.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 3)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.assertion: Aerobic exercise combined with dynamic resistance exercise can lower SBP by approximately 6.5 mmHg.
items.reason: The combination of aerobic and dynamic resistance exercises has been shown to have a greater BP-lowering effect than either alone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)
items.assertion: Nonpharmacologic interventions often enhance the effect of antihypertensive medications.
items.reason: This enhancement is especially well demonstrated for reductions in sodium intake.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why nonpharmacologic interventions generally enhance medication effects.
items.reference: Hypertension · NONPHARMACOLOGICAL INTERVENTIONS TO PREVENT AND TREAT HYPERTENSION (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Beta blockers are inferior to other antihypertensive drug classes for the prevention of stroke.
items.reason: Meta-analyses have identified diuretics as being the 'best in class' for first-step prevention of CVD.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Antihypertensive drug therapy should be used in addition to nonpharmacologic therapy for adults with stage 2 hypertension.
items.reason: Most individuals with stage 2 hypertension can be managed successfully with a single antihypertensive agent combined with nonpharmacologic therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as most require more than one agent.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: Starting with lower doses of a two-drug combination provides superior blood pressure control.
items.reason: This approach results in fewer side effects compared to using a single drug at a high dose.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: Nondihydropyridine CCBs should be used as first-line therapy for hypertension.
items.reason: They have a greater effect on the heart compared to dihydropyridines.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; nondihydropyridine CCBs are rarely employed unless there's another indication.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: An SBP goal of <120 mmHg is recommended for all adults with hypertension.
items.reason: Meta-analyses have shown greater benefit for lower achieved BP, especially starting from a higher baseline.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the general recommendation is an SBP goal of <130 mmHg.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: Lack of adherence to medication is a common reason for apparent resistant hypertension.
items.reason: Management starts with confirming adherence to the prescribed treatment regimen.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.assertion: ACEIs and ARBs are important in patients with proteinuria and advanced kidney disease.
items.reason: They reduce intraglomerular pressure by inducing efferent arteriolar dilatation, preserving glomerular function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)
items.assertion: Single-pill antihypertensive combinations are not useful for patients with kidney disease.
items.reason: Patients with kidney disease often require multiple medications, making single-pill combinations beneficial.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; single-pill combinations are indeed useful as they simplify medication regimens.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.assertion: Older adults with hypertension should be treated similarly to younger patients.
items.reason: RCT evidence for antihypertensive drug therapy generally comes from studies where the average age was close to 70 years at baseline.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why older adults can be treated similarly.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)
items.assertion: A J-shaped association between BP and CVD risk suggests that lower BP always reduces CVD risk.
items.reason: Randomized comparisons have shown prevention of CVD in those randomized to more intensive antihypertensive therapy at any point in the J-curve.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not support the assertion as it highlights that lower BP reduces risk only when managed properly.
items.reference: Hypertension · PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY TO PREVENT CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: PREVALENCE, AWARENESS, TREATMENT, AND CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION
items.assertion: The decrement in hypertension control was especially prominent in adults ≥75 years old.
items.reason: Older adults are more likely to have multiple comorbidities and complex medication regimens that can affect BP management.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not explicitly stated as the cause for the decrement in hypertension control among older adults.
items.reference: Hypertension · PREVALENCE, AWARENESS, TREATMENT, AND CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION

items.subtopic: IMPROVING THE CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION
items.assertion: Hypertension is among the most important, prevalent, modifiable, and cost-effective risk factors for CVD.
items.reason: Its detection, treatment, and control should be a high priority worldwide.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hypertension management is crucial.
items.reference: Hypertension · IMPROVING THE CONTROL OF HYPERTENSION
